tools.name: configuration_view
tools.description: Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML

minified.description: Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)

tools.name: events_list
tools.description: List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter events by field values (e.g. 'type=Warning', 'involvedObject.name=my-pod'). Supported fields: involvedObject.kind, involvedObject.name, involvedObject.namespace, involvedObject.uid, involvedObject.apiVersion, involvedObject.resourceVersion, involvedObject.fieldPath, reason, reportingComponent, source, type. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces

tools.name: namespaces_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter namespaces by field values (e.g. 'metadata.name=default', 'status.phase=Active'). Supported fields: metadata.name, status.phase. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

tools.name: nodes_log
tools.description: Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet

name.description: Name of the node to get logs from

query.description: query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")

tailLines.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)

tools.name: nodes_stats_summary
tools.description: Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics

name.description: Name of the node to get stats from

tools.name: nodes_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)

tools.name: pods_delete
tools.description: Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

name.description: Name of the Pod to delete

namespace.description: Namespace to delete the Pod from

tools.name: pods_exec
tools.description: Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command

command.description: Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]

container.description: Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod where the command will be executed

namespace.description: Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed

tools.name: pods_get
tools.description: Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod from

tools.name: pods_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_list_in_namespace
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_log
tools.description: Get the logs of a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

container.description: Name of the Pod container to get the logs from (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the logs from

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod logs from

tail.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, default: 100)

tools.name: pods_run
tools.description: Run a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided container image and optional name

image.description: Container Image to run in the Pod

name.description: Name of the Pod (Optional, random name if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to run the Pod in

port.description: TCP/IP port to expose from the Pod container (Optional, no port exposed if not provided)

tools.name: pods_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Pods in the all namespaces, the provided namespace, or the current namespace

all_namespaces.description: If true, list the resource consumption for all Pods in all namespaces. If false, list the resource consumption for Pods in the provided namespace or the current namespace

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Pods in the namespace if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pods resource consumption from (Optional, current namespace if not provided and all_namespaces is false)

tools.name: resources_create_or_update
tools.description:
Create or update a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing a YAML or JSON representation of the resource
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

resource.description: A JSON or YAML containing a representation of the Kubernetes resource. Should include top-level fields such as apiVersion,kind,metadata, and spec

tools.name: resources_delete
tools.description:
Delete a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

gracePeriodSeconds.description: Optional duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Value must be non-negative integer. The value zero indicates delete immediately. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to delete the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will delete resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_get
tools.description:
Get a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_list
tools.description:
List Kubernetes resources and objects in the current cluster by providing their apiVersion and kind and optionally the namespace and label selector
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resources (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter resources by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'metadata.name=myresource'). Supported fields vary by resource type. For Pods: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

kind.description: kind of the resources (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the resources by label

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resources from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will list resources from all namespaces

tools.name: resources_scale
tools.description: Get or update the scale of a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, name, and optionally the namespace. If the scale is set in the tool call, the scale will be updated to that value. Always returns the current scale of the resource

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are apps/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: StatefulSet, Deployment)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to get/update the namespaced resource scale from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get/update resource scale from configured namespace

scale.description: Optional scale to update the resources scale to. If not provided, will return the current scale of the resource, and not update it

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I changed
- Created canary Deployment canary-deployment-ns/engine-v2-1
  - Replicas: 2
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Container: rec-engine
  - Container port: 80
  - Security config: matched engine-v2-0 (pod securityContext: {})

- Updated Service canary-deployment-ns/recommendation-engine
  - Selector now: app=recommendation-engine (targets both v2.0 and v2.1)
  - Ports preserved: port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Current state
- Pods
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- With 2 pods in each Deployment and a shared Service selector, traffic will be approximately 50/50.

How to verify
- Endpoints show 4 backends:
  - kubectl get endpoints -n canary-deployment-ns recommendation-engine -o wide
- Watch requests split:
  - Send traffic and check logs
    - Stable: kubectl logs -n canary-deployment-ns -l app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0 --tail=10 -f
    - Canary: kubectl logs -n canary-deployment-ns -l app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.1 --tail=10 -f

Notes
- Service targetPort remains 8080 as before; canary matches the stable container port/security configuration.

Rollback/adjustments
- To send all traffic back to stable:
  - kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns patch svc recommendation-engine --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"recommendation-engine","version":"v2.0"}}}'
- To change the canary percentage, adjust replica counts (e.g., 3 stable, 1 canary ≈ 25% canary).

Steps.message:
namespace/canary-deployment-ns created
deployment.apps/engine-v2-0 created
service/recommendation-engine created

Done.

What I changed
- Created canary Deployment canary-deployment-ns/engine-v2-1
  - Replicas: 2
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Container: rec-engine
  - Container port: 80
  - Security config: matched engine-v2-0 (pod securityContext: {})

- Updated Service canary-deployment-ns/recommendation-engine
  - Selector now: app=recommendation-engine (targets both v2.0 and v2.1)
  - Ports preserved: port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Current state
- Pods
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- With 2 pods in each Deployment and a shared Service selector, traffic will be approximately 50/50.

How to verify
- Endpoints show 4 backends:
  - kubectl get endpoints -n canary-deployment-ns recommendation-engine -o wide
- Watch requests split:
  - Send traffic and check logs
    - Stable: kubectl logs -n canary-deployment-ns -l app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0 --tail=10 -f
    - Canary: kubectl logs -n canary-deployment-ns -l app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.1 --tail=10 -f

Notes
- Service targetPort remains 8080 as before; canary matches the stable container port/security configuration.

Rollback/adjustments
- To send all traffic back to stable:
  - kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns patch svc recommendation-engine --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"recommendation-engine","version":"v2.0"}}}'
- To change the canary percentage, adjust replica counts (e.g., 3 stable, 1 canary ≈ 25% canary).

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I changed
- Created canary Deployment canary-deployment-ns/engine-v2-1
  - Replicas: 2
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Container: rec-engine
  - Container port: 80
  - Security config: matched engine-v2-0 (pod securityContext: {})

- Updated Service canary-deployment-ns/recommendation-engine
  - Selector now: app=recommendation-engine (targets both v2.0 and v2.1)
  - Ports preserved: port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Current state
- Pods
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- With 2 pods in each Deployment and a shared Service selector, traffic will be approximately 50/50.

How to verify
- Endpoints show 4 backends:
  - kubectl get endpoints -n canary-deployment-ns recommendation-engine -o wide
- Watch requests split:
  - Send traffic and check logs
    - Stable: kubectl logs -n canary-deployment-ns -l app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0 --tail=10 -f
    - Canary: kubectl logs -n canary-deployment-ns -l app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.1 --tail=10 -f

Notes
- Service targetPort remains 8080 as before; canary matches the stable container port/security configuration.

Rollback/adjustments
- To send all traffic back to stable:
  - kubectl -n canary-deployment-ns patch svc recommendation-engine --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"recommendation-engine","version":"v2.0"}}}'
- To change the canary percentage, adjust replica counts (e.g., 3 stable, 1 canary ≈ 25% canary).

Steps.message:
Done.

- Pod: web-server
- Namespace: create-pod-test
- Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
- Status: Running, Ready=True
- Node: mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane
- Pod IP: 10.244.0.14
- Notes: The image needs write access to /tmp. I recreated the pod with readOnlyRootFilesystem disabled and set runAsUser/runAsGroup/fsGroup to 101 for compatibility. Readiness/liveness probes are HTTP on port 8080.

Verify:
- kubectl -n create-pod-test get pod web-server
- kubectl -n create-pod-test port-forward pod/web-server 8080:8080 (then curl http://127.0.0.1:8080)

results.taskOutput:
Short answer: Nothing’s wrong with the pod itself; your application is throwing division-by-zero errors.

What I see:
- Pod status: Running, Ready=True, restarts=0. No warning/error events.
- Logs from calc-app-pod: multiple lines like “failed with error: division by zero”.

Conclusion:
- Kubernetes is healthy for this pod. The issue is in calc-app.py (mounted from the calc-app-map ConfigMap) where a denominator sometimes becomes zero.

What to do next:
- Inspect the script from the ConfigMap:
  - kubectl -n calc-app get configmap calc-app-map -o yaml
- Add guards or input validation around divisions (e.g., check b != 0 or handle in try/except with a fallback).
- Update the ConfigMap with the fix, then restart the pod to pick up the new script:
  - kubectl -n calc-app apply -f <updated-configmap.yaml>
  - kubectl -n calc-app delete pod calc-app-pod

If you want, I can pull the current ConfigMap and help pinpoint the exact spot causing the zero division.

What to do next:
- Inspect the script from the ConfigMap:
  - kubectl -n calc-app get configmap calc-app-map -o yaml
- Add guards or input validation around divisions (e.g., check b != 0 or handle in try/except with a fallback).
- Update the ConfigMap with the fix, then restart the pod to pick up the new script:
  - kubectl -n calc-app apply -f <updated-configmap.yaml>
  - kubectl -n calc-app delete pod calc-app-pod

If you want, I can pull the current ConfigMap and help pinpoint the exact spot causing the zero division.

results.taskOutput:
Fixed.

What was wrong:
- The app Deployment’s container was started with: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, causing a CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the container command to start nginx in the foreground:
  - command: ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
- Removed the invalid args.

Verification:
- New pod is running: app-5cdf78665b-l8wgk 1/1 Running.
- Deployment app in crashloop-test is Available=True and Progressing=True with observedGeneration=2.

Optional next step:
- Your Deployment still has a stale kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration showing the old command. I can clean or update that to prevent future kubectl apply from reintroducing the bad command.

outputSteps.content:
Fixed.

What was wrong:
- The app Deployment’s container was started with: /bin/sh -c nonexistent_command, causing a CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the container command to start nginx in the foreground:
  - command: ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
- Removed the invalid args.

Verification:
- New pod is running: app-5cdf78665b-l8wgk 1/1 Running.
- Deployment app in crashloop-test is Available=True and Progressing=True with observedGeneration=2.

Optional next step:
- Your Deployment still has a stale kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration showing the old command. I can clean or update that to prevent future kubectl apply from reintroducing the bad command.

results.taskOutput:
I found the issue: the Deployment was using a nonexistent image tag (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag), causing ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff.

What I changed
- Updated the container image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest in deployment debug/app.

Current status
- Pods are now running:
  - debug/app-766ff8b47b-cwzcn: 1/1 Running
- Deployment debug/app is Available with 1 ready replica.

If you prefer a pinned version instead of latest, I can switch it to a specific known-good tag.

outputSteps.content:
I found the issue: the Deployment was using a nonexistent image tag (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag), causing ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff.

What I changed
- Updated the container image to quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest in deployment debug/app.

Current status
- Pods are now running:
  - debug/app-766ff8b47b-cwzcn: 1/1 Running
- Deployment debug/app is Available with 1 ready replica.

If you prefer a pinned version instead of latest, I can switch it to a specific known-good tag.

Steps.message:
deployment.apps/app condition met
Pod is stable. Verification successful.

results.taskOutput:
I investigated and fixed the issue. Summary:

Root cause
- The pod referenced PVC homepage-pvc which requested storageClassName=sc, but that StorageClass does not exist. This kept the PVC unbound and the pod Pending.

What I changed
- Deleted the bad PVC homepage-pvc.
- Created a new PVC homepage-pvc-local with the existing StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path), size 1Gi, RWO.
- Recreated pod homepage-pod to use homepage-pvc-local.

Current status
- Pod homepage-pod is Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.
- PVC homepage-pvc-local is Bound to a PV (provisioned by rancher.io/local-path).

How to verify
- kubectl -n homepage-ns get pod homepage-pod -o wide
- kubectl -n homepage-ns get pvc

Note
- I changed the claim name in the pod to homepage-pvc-local. If you prefer to keep the original claim name (homepage-pvc), I can recreate a PVC with that name using StorageClass standard and update the pod back accordingly.

Steps.message:
namespace/homepage-ns created
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc created
pod/homepage-pod created

I investigated and fixed the issue. Summary:

Root cause
- The pod referenced PVC homepage-pvc which requested storageClassName=sc, but that StorageClass does not exist. This kept the PVC unbound and the pod Pending.

What I changed
- Deleted the bad PVC homepage-pvc.
- Created a new PVC homepage-pvc-local with the existing StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path), size 1Gi, RWO.
- Recreated pod homepage-pod to use homepage-pvc-local.

Current status
- Pod homepage-pod is Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.
- PVC homepage-pvc-local is Bound to a PV (provisioned by rancher.io/local-path).

How to verify
- kubectl -n homepage-ns get pod homepage-pod -o wide
- kubectl -n homepage-ns get pvc

Note
- I changed the claim name in the pod to homepage-pvc-local. If you prefer to keep the original claim name (homepage-pvc), I can recreate a PVC with that name using StorageClass standard and update the pod back accordingly.

outputSteps.content:
I investigated and fixed the issue. Summary:

Root cause
- The pod referenced PVC homepage-pvc which requested storageClassName=sc, but that StorageClass does not exist. This kept the PVC unbound and the pod Pending.

What I changed
- Deleted the bad PVC homepage-pvc.
- Created a new PVC homepage-pvc-local with the existing StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path), size 1Gi, RWO.
- Recreated pod homepage-pod to use homepage-pvc-local.

Current status
- Pod homepage-pod is Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane.
- PVC homepage-pvc-local is Bound to a PV (provisioned by rancher.io/local-path).

How to verify
- kubectl -n homepage-ns get pod homepage-pod -o wide
- kubectl -n homepage-ns get pvc

Note
- I changed the claim name in the pod to homepage-pvc-local. If you prefer to keep the original claim name (homepage-pvc), I can recreate a PVC with that name using StorageClass standard and update the pod back accordingly.

results.taskOutput:
I found and fixed two issues in orders/webapp:

- Health checks and paths: The pod had liveness/readiness probes hitting /get_status and /is_ready on port 80, which don’t exist in the nginx-unprivileged image (returned 404), so the pod never became Ready.
- Wrong port: The nginx-unprivileged container listens on 8080 by default, but the Deployment and Service were configured for port 80.

What I changed:
- Deployment:
  - Updated readiness and liveness probes to GET / on port 8080.
  - Exposed container port 8080 (kept 80 as well; not harmful).
- Service:
  - Updated targetPort to 8080.

Current status:
- Deployment webapp is Available with 1/1 ready.
- Pod is Running and Ready.
- Service webapp has a healthy endpoint pointing to the pod on port 8080.

How to verify quickly:
- kubectl -n orders get deploy,po,svc
- kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access, I can create an Ingress or a LoadBalancer Service.

Steps.message:
namespace/orders created
deployment.apps/webapp created
service/webapp created
Waiting for pod to start and begin failing health checks...
deployment.apps/webapp condition met
Setup successful: Deployment is no longer available.

I found and fixed two issues in orders/webapp:

- Health checks and paths: The pod had liveness/readiness probes hitting /get_status and /is_ready on port 80, which don’t exist in the nginx-unprivileged image (returned 404), so the pod never became Ready.
- Wrong port: The nginx-unprivileged container listens on 8080 by default, but the Deployment and Service were configured for port 80.

What I changed:
- Deployment:
  - Updated readiness and liveness probes to GET / on port 8080.
  - Exposed container port 8080 (kept 80 as well; not harmful).
- Service:
  - Updated targetPort to 8080.

Current status:
- Deployment webapp is Available with 1/1 ready.
- Pod is Running and Ready.
- Service webapp has a healthy endpoint pointing to the pod on port 8080.

How to verify quickly:
- kubectl -n orders get deploy,po,svc
- kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access, I can create an Ingress or a LoadBalancer Service.

outputSteps.content:
I found and fixed two issues in orders/webapp:

- Health checks and paths: The pod had liveness/readiness probes hitting /get_status and /is_ready on port 80, which don’t exist in the nginx-unprivileged image (returned 404), so the pod never became Ready.
- Wrong port: The nginx-unprivileged container listens on 8080 by default, but the Deployment and Service were configured for port 80.

What I changed:
- Deployment:
  - Updated readiness and liveness probes to GET / on port 8080.
  - Exposed container port 8080 (kept 80 as well; not harmful).
- Service:
  - Updated targetPort to 8080.

Current status:
- Deployment webapp is Available with 1/1 ready.
- Pod is Running and Ready.
- Service webapp has a healthy endpoint pointing to the pod on port 8080.

How to verify quickly:
- kubectl -n orders get deploy,po,svc
- kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access, I can create an Ingress or a LoadBalancer Service.

Steps.message:
Checking if the pod is running and ready...
pod/webapp-5c679479cc-z49p2 condition met
Success: Pod is now Ready
Current liveness probe path: /
Current readiness probe path: /
Success: Both probe paths have been fixed
Success: Pod is stable with acceptable number of restarts

results.taskOutput:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup did not grant any permissions on core/v1 pods. It only allowed list on apps/deployments, so the service account pod-reader couldn’t list pods.

What I changed
- Updated Role pod-reader-role (in simple-rbac-setup) to include pods permissions:
  - apiGroup: "" (core)
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- Kept the existing deployments list permission.

Current Role rules
- pods: get, list, watch (core apiGroup "")
- deployments: list (apiGroup "apps")

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

Notes
- This grants access only within the simple-rbac-setup namespace (by design for a Role + RoleBinding). If you need cluster-wide pod listing, you’d use a ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding instead.

Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup did not grant any permissions on core/v1 pods. It only allowed list on apps/deployments, so the service account pod-reader couldn’t list pods.

What I changed
- Updated Role pod-reader-role (in simple-rbac-setup) to include pods permissions:
  - apiGroup: "" (core)
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- Kept the existing deployments list permission.

Current Role rules
- pods: get, list, watch (core apiGroup "")
- deployments: list (apiGroup "apps")

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

results.taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods:
  - Service selector was app=web
  - Deployment/Pods are labeled app=nginx
  - Result: the Service had no endpoints, so connections couldn’t reach any Pods.
- The Service targetPort was 80, but the nginx-unprivileged image listens on 8080 by default.

Cleanup
- Removed the temporary test pods used for verification.

Current status
- The nginx Service (ClusterIP) is now reachable inside the cluster at:
  - DNS: nginx.web.svc.cluster.local
  - ClusterIP: 10.96.216.252:80
- No NetworkPolicies or Ingress objects are blocking traffic.

Notes
- This Service is type ClusterIP, so it’s only accessible from within the cluster. If you need external access, I can set up a NodePort, LoadBalancer, or Ingress for you.

outputSteps.content:
Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods:
  - Service selector was app=web
  - Deployment/Pods are labeled app=nginx
  - Result: the Service had no endpoints, so connections couldn’t reach any Pods.
- The Service targetPort was 80, but the nginx-unprivileged image listens on 8080 by default.
  - Even with endpoints, traffic to targetPort 80 would fail because nothing listens on 80 in the Pod.

What I changed
- Updated the Service nginx in namespace web:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports.targetPort: 8080

Verification
- Endpoints now populated: 10.244.0.33:8080
- In-cluster test succeeded:
  - From a temporary pod in web: curl http://nginx returned HTTP/1.1 200 OK

Cleanup
- Removed the temporary test pods used for verification.

Current status
- The nginx Service (ClusterIP) is now reachable inside the cluster at:
  - DNS: nginx.web.svc.cluster.local
  - ClusterIP: 10.96.216.252:80
- No NetworkPolicies or Ingress objects are blocking traffic.

Notes
- This Service is type ClusterIP, so it’s only accessible from within the cluster. If you need external access, I can set up a NodePort, LoadBalancer, or Ingress for you.

Welcome to nginx!

If you see this page, nginx is successfully installed and working. Further configuration is required for the web server, reverse proxy, API gateway, load balancer, content cache, or other features.

For online documentation and support please refer to nginx.org.
To engage with the community please visit community.nginx.org.
For enterprise grade support, professional services, additional security features and capabilities please refer to f5.com/nginx.

Thank you for using nginx.

pod "test-connection" deleted from web namespace

results.taskOutput:
Here’s what I found and fixed.

Findings
- Pods not running: The Deployment had a nodeSelector environment=production-gpu. Your cluster’s only node does not have this label, so the scheduler couldn’t place the pods (FailedScheduling: didn’t match node affinity/selector).
- Connection refused: After getting pods scheduled, the containers were listening on 8080, but the Service was targeting port 80. So the service resolved, but connections to podIP:80 were refused.

What I changed
- Removed the nodeSelector from Deployment webshop-frontend/web-app-deployment so pods can schedule to the existing node.
- Updated Service webshop-frontend/web-app-service to targetPort: 8080 (port remains 80), matching what the container actually listens on.

Current status
- 2 pods are Running:
  - web-app-deployment-5fd65c8bdc-89jfr
  - web-app-deployment-5fd65c8bdc-7pwl6
- Service web-app-service has healthy endpoints and returns HTTP 200 from inside the cluster (verified via a temporary test pod).
- Your frontend calls to http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local should now succeed.

Recommendations and follow-ups
- If you intended to restrict scheduling to special nodes, either:
  - Reapply the nodeSelector and label the appropriate nodes: kubectl label node <node-name> environment=production-gpu
  - Or use node affinity/taints with matching tolerations explicitly.
- For clarity, consider updating the Deployment’s containerPort to 8080 to reflect the actual listening port (this is informational only; the Service now routes correctly).
- Add a readinessProbe on port 8080 so traffic only hits ready pods.

If you’d like, I can:
- Update containerPort to 8080 and add sensible readiness/liveness probes.
- Reintroduce a node constraint and label nodes accordingly.

content.text: Pod deleted successfully

Steps.message:
namespace/webshop-frontend created
service/web-app-service created
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment created
Waiting for resources to be created...
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment condition met
Warning: v1 Endpoints is deprecated in v1.33+; use discovery.k8s.io/v1 EndpointSlice
Setup successful: Service has no endpoints as expected

Here’s what I found and fixed.

Findings
- Pods not running: The Deployment had a nodeSelector environment=production-gpu. Your cluster’s only node does not have this label, so the scheduler couldn’t place the pods (FailedScheduling: didn’t match node affinity/selector).
- Connection refused: After getting pods scheduled, the containers were listening on 8080, but the Service was targeting port 80. So the service resolved, but connections to podIP:80 were refused.

What I changed
- Removed the nodeSelector from Deployment webshop-frontend/web-app-deployment so pods can schedule to the existing node.
- Updated Service webshop-frontend/web-app-service to targetPort: 8080 (port remains 80), matching what the container actually listens on.

Current status
- 2 pods are Running:
  - web-app-deployment-5fd65c8bdc-89jfr
  - web-app-deployment-5fd65c8bdc-7pwl6
- Service web-app-service has healthy endpoints and returns HTTP 200 from inside the cluster (verified via a temporary test pod).
- Your frontend calls to http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local should now succeed.

Recommendations and follow-ups
- If you intended to restrict scheduling to special nodes, either:
  - Reapply the nodeSelector and label the appropriate nodes: kubectl label node <node-name> environment=production-gpu
  - Or use node affinity/taints with matching tolerations explicitly.
- For clarity, consider updating the Deployment’s containerPort to 8080 to reflect the actual listening port (this is informational only; the Service now routes correctly).
- Add a readinessProbe on port 8080 so traffic only hits ready pods.

If you’d like, I can:
- Update containerPort to 8080 and add sensible readiness/liveness probes.
- Reintroduce a node constraint and label nodes accordingly.

outputSteps.content:
Here’s what I found and fixed.

Findings
- Pods not running: The Deployment had a nodeSelector environment=production-gpu. Your cluster’s only node does not have this label, so the scheduler couldn’t place the pods (FailedScheduling: didn’t match node affinity/selector).
- Connection refused: After getting pods scheduled, the containers were listening on 8080, but the Service was targeting port 80. So the service resolved, but connections to podIP:80 were refused.

What I changed
- Removed the nodeSelector from Deployment webshop-frontend/web-app-deployment so pods can schedule to the existing node.
- Updated Service webshop-frontend/web-app-service to targetPort: 8080 (port remains 80), matching what the container actually listens on.

Current status
- 2 pods are Running:
  - web-app-deployment-5fd65c8bdc-89jfr
  - web-app-deployment-5fd65c8bdc-7pwl6
- Service web-app-service has healthy endpoints and returns HTTP 200 from inside the cluster (verified via a temporary test pod).
- Your frontend calls to http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local should now succeed.

Recommendations and follow-ups
- If you intended to restrict scheduling to special nodes, either:
  - Reapply the nodeSelector and label the appropriate nodes: kubectl label node <node-name> environment=production-gpu
  - Or use node affinity/taints with matching tolerations explicitly.
- For clarity, consider updating the Deployment’s containerPort to 8080 to reflect the actual listening port (this is informational only; the Service now routes correctly).
- Add a readinessProbe on port 8080 so traffic only hits ready pods.

If you’d like, I can:
- Update containerPort to 8080 and add sensible readiness/liveness probes.
- Reintroduce a node constraint and label nodes accordingly.

outputSteps.content:
Done.

- Created namespace: multi-container-logging
- Created pod: communication-pod (in multi-container-logging) with:
  - Container web-server: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    - Exposes containerPort 8080
    - Readiness probe: HTTP GET / on port 8080 (pod becomes Ready when the web server serves traffic)
    - Mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
  - Container logger: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    - Command: sh -c 'touch /var/log/nginx/access.log; tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log'
    - Mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
  - Shared volume: logs-volume (emptyDir)

You can watch readiness: kubectl -n multi-container-logging get pod communication-pod -w
And view the logger output: kubectl -n multi-container-logging logs -f communication-pod -c logger

Steps.message:
Here’s the current resource usage for the control-plane (master) nodes.

Control-plane nodes detected:
- mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane

Node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane (allocatable: 4 CPU, ~15.6 GiB RAM, 154.9 GiB disk)
- CPU: ~130 mCPU (0.13 cores), ~3.3% of 4 cores
- Memory:
  - Usage: ~4.00 GiB (~25.6% of capacity)
  - Working set: ~1.11 GiB (~7.1% of capacity)
  - Swap: 0 in use
- Filesystem:
  - Root FS used: ~68.1 GiB / 154.9 GiB (~44%)
  - Image FS used: ~3.1 GiB
- Network (since node start):
  - RX: ~774 MiB
  - TX: ~10.3 MiB
- Top CPU consumers (instantaneous):
  - kube-apiserver: ~28–34 mCPU
  - etcd: ~20 mCPU
  - kube-controller-manager: ~10–12 mCPU
  - kube-scheduler: ~5–5.3 mCPU
  - Others (coredns, kube-proxy, ingress): ~1–1.2 mCPU each
- Top memory working set:
  - kube-apiserver: ~332 MiB
  - ingress-nginx controller: ~67 MiB
  - kube-controller-manager: ~60 MiB
  - etcd: ~56 MiB
  - kube-scheduler: ~26–27 MiB
  - coredns (each): ~17–20 MiB

Note: The Metrics Server isn’t available (kubectl top fails). These figures come from the kubelet Summary API. If you want kubectl top support or HPA-driven metrics, install Metrics Server and we can also set up a watch or alerts.

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I changed
- Enabled volume expansion on the default StorageClass “standard” (rancher.io/local-path).
- Increased the PersistentVolumeClaim storage-pvc (used by storage-pod) from 1Gi to 3Gi.

Current state
- PVC storage-pvc now requests 3Gi.
- Kubernetes events show: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC” (ExternalExpanding).
- PV and PVC capacity still report 1Gi while expansion is pending.

Notes and next steps
- Your cluster uses rancher.io/local-path. Some versions of this provisioner don’t support volume expansion. If expansion remains stuck:
  - Try restarting the Pod to trigger node-side filesystem resize if the driver supports it.
  - If expansion isn’t supported, we can create a new 3Gi PVC and migrate data, then re-create the Pod to use the new claim. Tell me if you want me to do this.

How to verify
- kubectl -n resize-pv get pvc storage-pvc
- kubectl -n resize-pv describe pvc storage-pvc (watch for capacity to become 3Gi and FileSystemResize* messages)

Want me to restart the pod or proceed with creating a new 3Gi PVC and migrating data if expansion doesn’t complete?

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I changed
- Enabled volume expansion on the default StorageClass “standard” (rancher.io/local-path).
- Increased the PersistentVolumeClaim storage-pvc (used by storage-pod) from 1Gi to 3Gi.

Current state
- PVC storage-pvc now requests 3Gi.
- Kubernetes events show: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC” (ExternalExpanding).
- PV and PVC capacity still report 1Gi while expansion is pending.

Notes and next steps
- Your cluster uses rancher.io/local-path. Some versions of this provisioner don’t support volume expansion. If expansion remains stuck:
  - Try restarting the Pod to trigger node-side filesystem resize if the driver supports it.
  - If expansion isn’t supported, we can create a new 3Gi PVC and migrate data, then re-create the Pod to use the new claim. Tell me if you want me to do this.

How to verify
- kubectl -n resize-pv get pvc storage-pvc
- kubectl -n resize-pv describe pvc storage-pvc (watch for capacity to become 3Gi and FileSystemResize* messages)

Want me to restart the pod or proceed with creating a new 3Gi PVC and migrating data if expansion doesn’t complete?

results.taskOutput:
I’ve created the requested multi-tenant setup. Summary of what’s in place and how it meets your requirements:

1) Namespaces
- Created: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Labeled to support NetworkPolicy selection and environment scoping

2) RBAC
- Developer “full access” to their own namespace:
  - RoleBindings in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie bind users (alice, bob, charlie) and their service accounts (alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa) to ClusterRole admin in their respective namespaces
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBindings in dev-shared bind users (alice, bob, charlie) and their SAs to ClusterRole view
- Staging/prod restricted:
  - No developer RoleBindings created in staging or prod (only cluster-admins can access)

Note: If you prefer stricter least-privilege than “admin” (which allows managing roles/rolebindings within the namespace), I can switch those to the standard “edit” ClusterRole. Say the word and I’ll update.

3) Resource Quotas
- dev-* (per-namespace): requests.cpu=2, limits.cpu=2, requests.memory=4Gi, limits.memory=4Gi, pods=10, services=5
- dev-shared: requests.cpu=4, limits.cpu=4, requests.memory=8Gi, limits.memory=8Gi, pods=20, services=10
- staging/prod: requests.cpu=8, limits.cpu=8, requests.memory=16Gi, limits.memory=16Gi, pods=50, services=20

4) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny applied in all six namespaces (Ingress + Egress)
- DNS egress allowed in all namespaces to CoreDNS/kube-dns on TCP/UDP 53 in kube-system
- Same-namespace communication allowed (Ingress + Egress) within each namespace
- Dev namespaces can egress to dev-shared only; cross-developer namespace traffic is blocked
- dev-shared allows ingress only from namespaces labeled env=dev (dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie)
- staging/prod isolated from dev: only same-namespace communication + DNS egress is allowed; no ingress from dev namespaces

5) Default Deny
- Implemented in every non-system namespace created here; kube-system is not restricted by these policies

Verification tips
- Auth:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'  (should be yes)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-bob get pods (should be no)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods (should be yes)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods (should be no)
- Network (requires NetworkPolicy-capable CNI such as Calico/Cilium):
  - From a pod in dev-alice, curl a pod/service in dev-bob (should fail)
  - From a pod in dev-alice, resolve DNS and reach a service in dev-shared (should work)
  - From any dev namespace to staging/prod (should fail)

Notes and optional next steps
- If any distro uses different CoreDNS labels, update the DNS NetworkPolicy podSelector accordingly.
- Consider adding LimitRange per namespace to set default pod/container requests/limits that align with the quotas.
- If developers should not be able to alter RBAC in their namespaces, replace ClusterRole admin with edit in the RoleBindings.

Everything described above has been applied to the cluster. If you want me to switch own-namespace privileges from admin to edit, or broaden “system services” egress beyond DNS, I can update the policies accordingly.

arguments.resource:
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-alice
  labels:
    env: dev
    tenant: developer
    owner: alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-bob
  labels:
    env: dev
    tenant: developer
    owner: bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-charlie
  labels:
    env: dev
    tenant: developer
    owner: charlie
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-shared
  labels:
    env: shared
    tenant: shared
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: staging
  labels:
    env: staging
    tenant: restricted
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: prod
  labels:
    env: prod
    tenant: restricted
---
# Service Accounts for each developer in their own namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# RBAC: grant full access in own namespace (admin) to users and their SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: charlie-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
# RBAC: read-only access to dev-shared for users and their SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view-users
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view-serviceaccounts
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# Resource Quotas
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-standard
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-standard
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-standard
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-shared
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    limits.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-staging
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-prod
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"

Steps.message:
Setting up cluster state for dev environment eval...
Setup completed successfully

I’ve created the requested multi-tenant setup. Summary of what’s in place and how it meets your requirements:

1) Namespaces
- Created: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Labeled to support NetworkPolicy selection and environment scoping

2) RBAC
- Developer “full access” to their own namespace:
  - RoleBindings in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie bind users (alice, bob, charlie) and their service accounts (alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa) to ClusterRole admin in their respective namespaces
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBindings in dev-shared bind users (alice, bob, charlie) and their SAs to ClusterRole view
- Staging/prod restricted:
  - No developer RoleBindings created in staging or prod (only cluster-admins can access)

Note: If you prefer stricter least-privilege than “admin” (which allows managing roles/rolebindings within the namespace), I can switch those to the standard “edit” ClusterRole. Say the word and I’ll update.

3) Resource Quotas
- dev-* (per-namespace): requests.cpu=2, limits.cpu=2, requests.memory=4Gi, limits.memory=4Gi, pods=10, services=5
- dev-shared: requests.cpu=4, limits.cpu=4, requests.memory=8Gi, limits.memory=8Gi, pods=20, services=10
- staging/prod: requests.cpu=8, limits.cpu=8, requests.memory=16Gi, limits.memory=16Gi, pods=50, services=20

4) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny applied in all six namespaces (Ingress + Egress)
- DNS egress allowed in all namespaces to CoreDNS/kube-dns on TCP/UDP 53 in kube-system
- Same-namespace communication allowed (Ingress + Egress) within each namespace
- Dev namespaces can egress to dev-shared only; cross-developer namespace traffic is blocked
- dev-shared allows ingress only from namespaces labeled env=dev (dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie)
- staging/prod isolated from dev: only same-namespace communication + DNS egress is allowed; no ingress from dev namespaces

5) Default Deny
- Implemented in every non-system namespace created here; kube-system is not restricted by these policies

Verification tips
- Auth:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'  (should be yes)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-bob get pods (should be no)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods (should be yes)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods (should be no)
- Network (requires NetworkPolicy-capable CNI such as Calico/Cilium):
  - From a pod in dev-alice, curl a pod/service in dev-bob (should fail)
  - From a pod in dev-alice, resolve DNS and reach a service in dev-shared (should work)
  - From any dev namespace to staging/prod (should fail)

Notes and optional next steps
- If any distro uses different CoreDNS labels, update the DNS NetworkPolicy podSelector accordingly.
- Consider adding LimitRange per namespace to set default pod/container requests/limits that align with the quotas.
- If developers should not be able to alter RBAC in their namespaces, replace ClusterRole admin with edit in the RoleBindings.

Everything described above has been applied to the cluster. If you want me to switch own-namespace privileges from admin to edit, or broaden “system services” egress beyond DNS, I can update the policies accordingly.

apiVersion: v1

metadata:

  labels:
    env: prod
    tenant: restricted
---
# Service Accounts for each developer in their own namespaces

kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1

metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# RBAC: grant full access in own namespace (admin) to users and their SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1

  - kind: ServiceAccount

toolCall.title: resources_create_or_update

rawInput.resource:
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-alice
  labels:
    env: dev
    tenant: developer
    owner: alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-bob
  labels:
    env: dev
    tenant: developer
    owner: bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-charlie
  labels:
    env: dev
    tenant: developer
    owner: charlie
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-shared
  labels:
    env: shared
    tenant: shared
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: staging
  labels:
    env: staging
    tenant: restricted
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: prod
  labels:
    env: prod
    tenant: restricted
---
# Service Accounts for each developer in their own namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# RBAC: grant full access in own namespace (admin) to users and their SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: charlie-admin-binding
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
# RBAC: read-only access to dev-shared for users and their SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view-users
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view-serviceaccounts
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# Resource Quotas
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-standard
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-standard
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-standard
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-shared
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    limits.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-staging
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-prod
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
# Network Policies: Default deny + allow DNS egress + allow same-namespace; dev namespaces also allow egress to dev-shared; dev-shared allows ingress from dev namespaces
# Helper: function to repeat similar policies is not available, so we list them explicitly
# dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: kube-dns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: coredns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: shared
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# dev-bob
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: kube-dns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: coredns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: shared
  policyTypes:

outputSteps.content:
I’ve created the requested multi-tenant setup. Summary of what’s in place and how it meets your requirements:

1) Namespaces
- Created: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Labeled to support NetworkPolicy selection and environment scoping

2) RBAC
- Developer “full access” to their own namespace:
  - RoleBindings in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie bind users (alice, bob, charlie) and their service accounts (alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa) to ClusterRole admin in their respective namespaces
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBindings in dev-shared bind users (alice, bob, charlie) and their SAs to ClusterRole view
- Staging/prod restricted:
  - No developer RoleBindings created in staging or prod (only cluster-admins can access)

Note: If you prefer stricter least-privilege than “admin” (which allows managing roles/rolebindings within the namespace), I can switch those to the standard “edit” ClusterRole. Say the word and I’ll update.

3) Resource Quotas
- dev-* (per-namespace): requests.cpu=2, limits.cpu=2, requests.memory=4Gi, limits.memory=4Gi, pods=10, services=5
- dev-shared: requests.cpu=4, limits.cpu=4, requests.memory=8Gi, limits.memory=8Gi, pods=20, services=10
- staging/prod: requests.cpu=8, limits.cpu=8, requests.memory=16Gi, limits.memory=16Gi, pods=50, services=20

4) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny applied in all six namespaces (Ingress + Egress)
- DNS egress allowed in all namespaces to CoreDNS/kube-dns on TCP/UDP 53 in kube-system
- Same-namespace communication allowed (Ingress + Egress) within each namespace
- Dev namespaces can egress to dev-shared only; cross-developer namespace traffic is blocked
- dev-shared allows ingress only from namespaces labeled env=dev (dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie)
- staging/prod isolated from dev: only same-namespace communication + DNS egress is allowed; no ingress from dev namespaces

5) Default Deny
- Implemented in every non-system namespace created here; kube-system is not restricted by these policies

Verification tips
- Auth:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'  (should be yes)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-bob get pods (should be no)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods (should be yes)
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods (should be no)
- Network (requires NetworkPolicy-capable CNI such as Calico/Cilium):
  - From a pod in dev-alice, curl a pod/service in dev-bob (should fail)
  - From a pod in dev-alice, resolve DNS and reach a service in dev-shared (should work)
  - From any dev namespace to staging/prod (should fail)

Notes and optional next steps
- If any distro uses different CoreDNS labels, update the DNS NetworkPolicy podSelector accordingly.
- Consider adding LimitRange per namespace to set default pod/container requests/limits that align with the quotas.
- If developers should not be able to alter RBAC in their namespaces, replace ClusterRole admin with edit in the RoleBindings.

Everything described above has been applied to the cluster. If you want me to switch own-namespace privileges from admin to edit, or broaden “system services” egress beyond DNS, I can update the policies accordingly.

content.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  annotations:
    deployment.kubernetes.io/revision: "1"
  creationTimestamp: "2026-06-15T11:55:06Z"
  generation: 1
  labels:
    app: payment-service
    team: platform
  name: payment-service
  namespace: ssa-test
  resourceVersion: "6732"
  uid: f3a670e6-2e81-485a-a257-e593ed9a7ce4
spec:
  progressDeadlineSeconds: 600
  replicas: 2
  revisionHistoryLimit: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payment-service
  strategy:
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 25%
      maxUnavailable: 25%
    type: RollingUpdate
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: payment-service
    spec:

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I applied:
- Namespace statefulset-test
- Headless Service db (required by StatefulSet)
- StatefulSet db with 3 replicas
  - volumeClaimTemplates: 1Gi PVC per pod, mounted at /data
  - initContainer writes initial_data to /data/test if it doesn’t already exist (so data isn’t overwritten on restarts)
- Scaled StatefulSet db back down to 1 replica

Notes:
- The PVCs created for db-0, db-1, db-2 are retained when scaling down, so data persists and will be reused if you scale back up.

Quick verification (optional):
- kubectl -n statefulset-test get sts db,pods,pvc
- kubectl -n statefulset-test exec db-0 -- sh -c 'cat /data/test' (should print: initial_data)

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I applied:
- Namespace statefulset-test
- Headless Service db (required by StatefulSet)
- StatefulSet db with 3 replicas
  - volumeClaimTemplates: 1Gi PVC per pod, mounted at /data
  - initContainer writes initial_data to /data/test if it doesn’t already exist (so data isn’t overwritten on restarts)
- Scaled StatefulSet db back down to 1 replica

Notes:
- The PVCs created for db-0, db-1, db-2 are retained when scaling down, so data persists and will be reused if you scale back up.

Quick verification (optional):
- kubectl -n statefulset-test get sts db,pods,pvc
- kubectl -n statefulset-test exec db-0 -- sh -c 'cat /data/test' (should print: initial_data)

Steps.message:
Verifying old pods are deleted
Old pods are deleted
Verifying StatefulSet replica count
StatefulSet is running with 1 replicas
Defaulted container "db" out of: db, init-data (init)
